title: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
authors: Elizabeth R. Unger, Jin-Mann S. Lin, Jeanne Bertolli

items.subtopic: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Prevalence and Economic Impact of ME/CFS
items.content: ME/CFS is a significant chronic illness with substantial prevalence and economic burden. It affects between 836,000 to 3.3 million Americans, yet over 80% remain undiagnosed by healthcare providers. The condition incurs annual costs ranging from $18 to $51 billion due to medical expenses and lost income.
keyPoints: Prevalence in the U.S.: between 836,000 and 3.3 million
Over 80% of those meeting criteria remain undiagnosed
Annual economic cost: $18 to $51 billion
Three to four times more common in women than men
Highest prevalence among ages 40–50
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Demographic and Socioeconomic Characteristics of ME/CFS Patients
items.content: ME/CFS affects a broad demographic, with higher prevalence in women and those aged 40–50. It impacts all races and ethnicities, with some evidence suggesting increased risk among socioeconomically disadvantaged groups.
keyPoints: Three to four times more common in women than men
Highest prevalence in ages 40–50
Affects all races and ethnicities
Potential increased risk in socioeconomically disadvantaged groups
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Infectious Agents as Risk Factors for ME/CFS
items.content: A variety of infectious agents, including viral and nonviral pathogens such as Epstein-Barr virus, Ross River virus, Coxiella burnettii (Q fever), Ebola virus, SARS-CoV-1, Giardia, and recently SARS-CoV-2, have been associated with postinfectious fatiguing illness resembling ME/CFS. While most individuals recover from these infections, approximately 10% remain ill for six months or longer.
keyPoints: Infectious agents linked to ME/CFS include Epstein-Barr virus, Ross River virus, Coxiella burnettii (Q fever), Ebola virus, SARS-CoV-1, Giardia, and SARS-CoV-2.
~10% of those infected with these pathogens remain ill for ≥6 months.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · RISK FACTORS AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Complex Pathophysiology of ME/CFS
items.content: ME/CFS is a multifaceted disorder characterized by alterations in multiple interrelated homeostatic systems. Although evidence for immunologic dysfunction, such as modest elevations in antinuclear antibodies and altered cytokine production, has been observed, these findings are inconsistent and not present in most patients. Other studies have noted nonspecific changes in brain structures, autonomic nervous system dysfunction, HPA axis abnormalities, altered metabolism, and intestinal microbiome dysbiosis; however, none of these findings are consistent enough for diagnostic use.
keyPoints: ME/CFS involves alterations in multiple interrelated homeostatic systems.
Immunologic dysfunction evidence is inconsistent and not present in most patients.
Nonspecific changes include regional brain structure alterations, autonomic nervous system dysfunction, HPA axis abnormalities, altered metabolism, and intestinal microbiome dysbiosis.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · RISK FACTORS AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
items.title: Recognizing ME/CFS: Key Symptoms and Duration
items.content: Diagnosis of ME/CFS is based on specific symptoms persisting for more than six months, including profound fatigue not alleviated by rest, postexertional malaise (PEM), unrefreshing sleep, cognitive impairment, or orthostatic intolerance.
keyPoints: Symptoms must last >6 months
Fatigue is new onset and profound, not relieved by rest
Postexertional malaise worsens symptoms after exertion
Unrefreshing sleep, cognitive impairment, or orthostatic intolerance should be present at least half the time with moderate intensity
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
items.title: Evaluating ME/CFS: Early Consideration and Patient Interaction
items.content: Consider ME/CFS in patients with consistent symptoms persisting >1 month, and initiate evaluation/supportive care as early as 4-6 weeks after onset. Engage patients to describe fatigue and PEM accurately by comparing current activity levels to pre-illness levels.
keyPoints: Evaluate for ME/CFS if symptoms persist >1 month
Begin supportive care at 4-6 weeks post-onset
Assess patient's activity levels and recovery time after exertion
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
items.title: Differential Diagnosis in ME/CFS: Comprehensive Evaluation
items.content: While the IOM criteria do not exclude other conditions, a thorough clinical evaluation is necessary to identify and treat illnesses that could explain or contribute to symptoms. This includes reviewing medical history, performing physical exams, and conducting routine laboratory tests.
keyPoints: Conduct comprehensive clinical evaluation
Review family and medical history for infections, traumas, surgeries, and exposures
Perform physical examination including POTS assessment
Screen for depression and anxiety
Routine lab tests should include CBC, ESR, electrolytes, renal function, liver function, CRP, thyroid function, iron studies, celiac disease screening, and urinalysis
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT
items.title: Approach to Fatigue in ME/CFS: Importance of Tailored Management and Awareness of Comorbidities
items.content: In managing fatigue associated with Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS), a careful selection of workups and referrals based on patient history, symptoms, and initial lab results is crucial. Recognizing comorbid conditions can enhance quality of life through targeted management strategies.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis for fatigue should be carefully chosen based on patient's history, new or worsening symptoms, and initial lab tests.
Management of ME/CFS involves individualized plans addressing the most problematic symptoms.
Nonpharmacologic therapies (e.g., sleep hygiene, massage) may improve disturbed sleep and pain.
Patients with ME/CFS are more sensitive to medications; start at lower doses and increase slowly.
Narcotics should be avoided in treatment of ME/CFS.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT
items.title: Managing Activity Levels in ME/CFS to Prevent 'Push and Crash' Cycle
items.content: Educating patients with ME/CFS about Post-Exertional Malaise (PEM) is essential to prevent the detrimental cycle of overexertion followed by relapse. Implementing pacing strategies helps maintain activity levels, minimizing deconditioning.
keyPoints: Avoid overexertion during 'good days' to prevent 'push and crash' cycles.
Activity management (pacing) is crucial in limiting PEM.
Maintain tolerated activity levels to minimize deconditioning.
Gradually increase activity as tolerated.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following statements best describes the prevalence of ME/CFS in the United States?
options: Between 836,000 and 3.3 million Americans are estimated to have ME/CFS.
Less than 500,000 Americans are affected by ME/CFS.
ME/CFS affects more men than women.
The prevalence of ME/CFS is highest among children under the age of 10.
items.explanation: Population-based studies estimate that between 836,000 and 3.3 million Americans have ME/CFS.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the diagnosis of ME/CFS?
options: A simple diagnostic test for ME/CFS exists and is widely used.
≥80% of those meeting criteria for ME/CFS have been diagnosed by a health care provider.
Evaluation by an experienced clinician is required for case identification due to the absence of a simple diagnostic test.
ME/CFS can be accurately diagnosed through self-reporting without clinical evaluation.
items.explanation: In the absence of a simple diagnostic test, evaluation by an experienced clinician is required for case identification.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which demographic group is most commonly affected by ME/CFS?
options: ME/CFS is equally common in men and women.
The highest prevalence of ME/CFS is among those 40–50 years of age, but it affects all ages.
Children under the age of 10 are the most affected group.
ME/CFS predominantly affects elderly individuals over the age of 65.
items.explanation: The highest prevalence is among those 40–50 years of age, but the illness affects a broad age range including children and adolescents.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.explanation: Influenza virus is not mentioned in the passage as being associated with ME/CFS.

items.explanation: High blood pressure is not mentioned as a stressor in the passage.

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
items.question: Which symptom is defined by a relapse in symptoms triggered by exertion that would not have been problematic before the onset of ME/CFS?
items.explanation: Postexertional malaise is characterized by a relapse in symptoms after exertion that was not problematic before the illness.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
items.question: What is the minimum duration for which symptoms must persist to meet the IOM diagnostic criteria for ME/CFS?
items.explanation: The illness must be present at least 6 months according to the IOM diagnostic criteria.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
items.question: Which symptom must be present at least half the time and with moderate intensity according to the IOM criteria?
items.explanation: Both cognitive impairment or orthostatic intolerance must be present at least half the time and with moderate intensity.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.question: What is the role of routine laboratory tests in diagnosing ME/CFS?
options: To confirm the diagnosis of ME/CFS
To identify other illnesses that could explain symptoms
None, as they are not useful
items.explanation: Routine laboratory tests help in identifying other illnesses that could explain the patient's symptoms.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | TABLE 461-1 2015 Institute of Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT
items.question: Which of the following medications should be avoided in patients with ME/CFS due to potential increased sensitivity?
items.explanation: Narcotics should be avoided as patients with ME/CFS may have increased sensitivity to medications.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is a recommended nonpharmacologic therapy for managing disturbed sleep in ME/CFS?
items.explanation: Nonpharmacologic therapies such as acupuncture can be beneficial for managing disturbed sleep.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT
items.question: What is the term used to describe the harmful cycle of overexertion followed by relapse in ME/CFS patients?
items.explanation: 'Push and crash' describes the cycle of overexertion during good days leading to relapse.
items.reference: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis Chronic Fatigue Syndrome · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND COMORBID CONDITIONS | MANAGEMENT

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the economic impact of ME/CFS is significant.

items.explanation: While patients may describe an initiating factor, frequently no such factor is recognized.
